supported_content: Stochastic Path Sampler For Lattice Field Theory (2026), authors: Shiyang Chen; Moxian Qian; Gert Aarts; Biagio Lucini; Kai Zhou
Data-free sampling of an unnormalized lattice target near criticality, where ordinary chains develop long autocorrelation times.
Learn paired forward and auxiliary backward Langevin paths by minimizing a path-space variational free energy / entropy-production upper bound.
Corrected SPS observables agree with HMC in the tested 2D phi4 systems and show shorter autocorrelation near the pseudocritical region.
Uncorrected tails and multimodal regions deviate; finite network capacity, terminal diffusion and support coverage remain practical limits, and the reported timing is hardware/setup specific.
Path Integral Sampler: a stochastic control approach for sampling (2022), authors: Zhang, Qinsheng and Chen, Yongxin
Construct an efficient sampler for an unnormalized target by controlling a stochastic path rather than fitting a terminal density alone.
Formulate sampling as a Schrodinger-bridge / stochastic optimal-control problem and learn a neural control for a reference diffusion.
The learned path sampler is competitive with the reported MCMC, SMC and normalizing-flow baselines on its benchmark suite.
Training overhead, time discretization and a suboptimal learned control can produce weight variance and residual bias if correction is omitted.
Denoising Diffusion Samplers (2023), authors: Vargas, Francisco and Grathwohl, Will and Doucet, Arnaud
Sample unnormalized targets with a diffusion process without requiring target-distributed training data.
Train a denoising diffusion sampler through a path-space objective and construct tractable forward/reverse trajectory densities.
The method performs strongly on the paper's multimodal and Bayesian benchmarks relative to selected diffusion and SMC baselines.
Optimization and diffusion discretization are sensitive, and the paper identifies smoothing/objective design as open improvements.
Transport meets Variational Inference: Controlled Monte Carlo Diffusions (2024), authors: Vargas, Francisco and Padhy, Shreyas and Blessing, Denis and N \"u sken, Nikolas
Reduce the variance and instability of Monte Carlo diffusion samplers built from a fixed annealing process.
Jointly adapt forward and backward controlled diffusions using path-space divergences linked to transport and nonequilibrium identities.
Controlled Monte Carlo Diffusions improve the reported sampling metrics across the benchmark suite.
Performance still depends on annealing schedule, divergence choice, numerical SDE solution and learned-control quality.
NETS: A Non-Equilibrium Transport Sampler (2024), authors: Albergo, Michael S. and Vanden-Eijnden, Eric
Annealed importance sampling can have high work/weight variance when the fixed protocol transports poorly between prior and target.
NETS augments nonequilibrium Langevin transport with a learned drift chosen to reduce estimator variance while retaining the nonequilibrium identity.
The learned transport improves effective sample size on the reported molecular and lattice phi4 examples.
Drift capacity, protocol, diffusion strength, training cost and remaining weight variance determine practical gains.
Stochastic normalizing flows for lattice field theory (2022), authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada; Marco Panero
Improve lattice-field sampling by combining learned invertible maps with robust Monte Carlo transitions.
Interleave normalizing-flow layers and Monte Carlo updates to form a stochastic normalizing flow for 2D phi4 theory.
The short study reports improved sampling efficiency over its purely learned or stochastic components.
The paper is a compact proceedings report in a low-dimensional scalar benchmark, so broad scaling claims are unsupported.
Stochastic normalizing flows as non-equilibrium transformations (2022), authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada; Marco Panero
Normalizing flows can be hard to train globally, while nonequilibrium transformations can require long protocols.
Unify invertible flow layers and stochastic nonequilibrium transitions within a path-probability/Jarzynski framework.
Hybrid stochastic normalizing flows improve free-energy estimation and sampling efficiency in the reported lattice examples.
Efficiency trades off network training, protocol length, stochastic steps and residual work variance.
Sampling Nambu-Goto theory using Normalizing Flows (2023), authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada
Obtain a scalable numerical sampler for lattice Nambu-Goto effective string theory where continuous normalizing flows scale poorly.
Use a physics-informed stochastic normalizing flow that interleaves learned maps with nonequilibrium stochastic updates and evaluates nonequilibrium work.
The proof-of-concept reproduces known partition-function behavior and reports agreement with a conjectured string-width coefficient over 314 parameter combinations.
This proceedings study addresses a specific effective-string regime; it does not establish general lattice-field or QCD scaling.
Flow-based generative models for Markov chain Monte Carlo in lattice field theory (2019), authors: Albergo, M. S. and Kanwar, G. and Shanahan, P. E.
Critical slowing makes conventional lattice MCMC expensive near phase transitions.
Train a normalizing flow without target samples and use independent flow proposals in a Metropolis-Hastings chain.
In 2D phi4, trained flow proposals reproduce observables and can eliminate sampling-stage critical slowing at fixed acceptance.
Training took substantial GPU time in this proof of principle; architecture scaling, memory and extension to QCD were not established.
Scalable Generative Sampling and Multilevel Estimation for Lattice Field Theories Near Criticality (2026), authors: A. Singha; J. Kauffmann; E. Cellini; K. Jansen; S. Nakajima
Overcome critical slowing and poor large-volume scaling by representing long- and short-distance modes separately.
Factor the target coarse-to-fine; use conditional Gaussian mixtures and masked continuous normalizing flows while preserving coarse sites exactly.
On critical 2D phi4, the method reports much lower autocorrelation than HMC, high IS efficiency, consistent observables and MLMC variance reduction.
ESS still falls at the largest volumes, fine-level quality limits MLMC gains, and extension to group-valued gauge theories is open.

Flow-based sampling for fermionic lattice field theories (2021), authors: Michael S. Albergo; Gurtej Kanwar; Sébastien Racanière; Danilo J. Rezende; Julian M. Urban; Denis Boyda; Kyle Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Phiala E. Shanahan
Extend flow-based sampling from bosonic fields to theories with dynamical fermions and nonlocal determinants.
Construct joint, marginal and pseudofermion-conditioned flow models and test them in a 2D Yukawa system.
The proof of principle reproduces observables in a dynamical-fermion lattice example.
The study does not establish realistic 4D QCD scaling; pseudofermion variance and determinant/nonlocal costs remain central.
Generative Diffusion Models for Lattice Field Theory (2023), authors: Lingxiao Wang; Gert Aarts; Kai Zhou
Connect diffusion generative models to lattice stochastic quantization and test whether they can serve as global proposals.
Learn the reverse diffusion / score process in a toy model and 2D phi4 theory.
The model reproduces tested observables and can reduce chain autocorrelation when used as a global sampler.
The demonstrations are toy/2D, require training configurations, and leave data-free training, gauge fields, fermions and sign problems open.
Physics-Conditioned Diffusion Models for Lattice Gauge Theory (2025), authors: Qianteng Zhu; Gert Aarts; Wei Wang; Kai Zhou; Lingxiao Wang
Generate gauge configurations across couplings while respecting gauge structure and reducing topological freezing.
Train a physics-conditioned diffusion model and use annealed Langevin / Metropolis-adjusted Langevin updates in 2D U(1).
The conditioned model transfers across couplings/lattice settings and improves topological exploration in the reported tests.
Training-data topology can bias the model; non-Abelian groups, fermion nonlocality and large-volume cost remain open.
SCORENF: Score-based Normalizing Flows for Sampling Unnormalized distributions (2025), authors: Vikas Kanaujia; Vipul Arora
Avoid mode collapse and mode covering when learning unnormalized multimodal targets from limited data.
Train an invertible normalizing flow with score-based and reverse-KL terms, then use it as an IMH proposal.
ScoreNF improves the reported NLL/RNLL balance, ESS and acceptance on Gaussian mixtures and a 64-dimensional phi4 example.
It still relies on target samples for score estimation and is only tested on modest systems; larger symmetric systems are future work.
Diffusion model for SU(N) gauge theories (2026), authors: Javad Komijani; Marina K. Marinkovic; Lara Turgut
Build diffusion samplers directly on non-Abelian SU(N) gauge manifolds while preserving gauge symmetry.
Use implicit score matching with a gauge-equivariant U-Net and a predictor-corrector/Hamiltonian refinement.
The paper demonstrates 2D and 4D SU(3)-family gauge sampling with improved observables after correction.
Correction raises computational cost; large-volume/continuum efficiency and richer gauge dynamics remain unresolved.

Equivariant flow-based sampling for lattice gauge theory (2020), authors: Gurtej Kanwar; Michael S. Albergo; Denis Boyda; K. Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Sébastien Racanière; Danilo Jimenez Rezende; Phiala E. Shanahan
Topological freezing makes gauge-field MCMC inefficient toward the continuum limit.
Construct gauge-equivariant normalizing-flow coupling layers and use them as independence-Metropolis proposals in 2D U(1).
For the tested beta=7, 16x16 setup, topological autocorrelation is far smaller than HMC/heat bath and the paper reports large efficiency gains for topological observables.
This is an Abelian 2D proof of principle; expressive invertible kernels for non-Abelian theories and full scaling remained future work.

Flow-based sampling in the lattice Schwinger model at criticality (2022), authors: Albergo, Michael S. and Boyda, Denis and Cranmer, Kyle and Hackett, Daniel C. and Kanwar, Gurtej and Racani \`e re, S \'e bastien and Rezende, Danilo J. and Romero-L \'o pez, Fernando and Shanahan, Phiala E. and Urban, Julian M.
Sample all topological sectors of the critical lattice Schwinger model when conventional HMC streams can freeze and underestimate uncertainty.
Train a symmetry-aware flow and use it inside an exact flow-based MCMC sampler for the fermionic gauge theory.
At the tested critical parameters, the paper reports frequent topological tunneling and an estimated sampling-efficiency advantage of more than three orders of magnitude over its HMC setup.
The comparison excludes full setup and raw computational costs, and exact determinant evaluation is not scalable to large volumes or higher dimensions.
Scaling Up Machine Learning For Quantum Field Theory with Equivariant Continuous Flows (2021), authors: Pim de Haan and Corrado Rainone and Miranda C. N. Cheng and Roberto Bondesan

anchor: PDF p.1 abstract and pp.1-2 introduction
supported_content: Improve the scaling and sampling efficiency of flow models as lattice size grows.
Use a shallow symmetry-aware continuous normalizing flow based on a neural ODE and handcrafted equivariant features.

anchor: PDF p.1 abstract and pp.5-7 experiments
supported_content: On the paper's largest 32 by 32 phi4 lattice, the abstract reports ESS increasing from 1 percent for the RealNVP baseline to 66 percent for the proposed model.
The tests use a simple scalar theory; systematic scaling and locally gauge-equivariant extensions needed for QCD remain future work.

Estimation of Thermodynamic Observables in Lattice Field Theories with Deep Generative Models (2021), authors: Nicoli, Kim A. and Anders, Christopher J. and Funcke, Lena and Hartung, Tobias and Jansen, Karl and Kessel, Pan and Nakajima, Shinichi and Stornati, Paolo
Estimate an absolute free energy without integrating many MCMC free-energy differences and accumulating trajectory errors.
Train a tractable normalizing-flow density and use model samples and density ratios to estimate the partition function and free energy directly.
For the tested two-dimensional phi4 systems, the direct flow estimates agree with the paper's analytic and MCMC comparisons.
The demonstration is restricted to two-dimensional phi4; scaling to four-dimensional gauge theories is left to future work.
Simulating the Hubbard Model with Equivariant Normalizing Flows (2025), authors: Dominic Schuh; Janik Kreit; Evan Berkowitz; Lena Funcke; Thomas Luu; Kim A. Nicoli; Marcel Rodekamp
Mitigate severe ergodicity barriers encountered by HMC in small Hubbard-model systems.
Construct normalizing flows equivariant under selected Hubbard symmetries and apply NeuralMCMC metropolization to the generated proposals.
For the tested 2 by 1 and 2 by 2 systems, equivariance reduces training cost and improves acceptance and autocorrelation relative to the non-equivariant model.
This is a small-system proof of concept; preserving bijectivity under canonicalization and scaling to larger temporal extent remain unresolved.

Introduction to Normalizing Flows for Lattice Field Theory (2021), authors: Michael S. Albergo; Denis Boyda; Daniel C. Hackett; Gurtej Kanwar; Kyle Cranmer; Sébastien Racanière; Danilo Jimenez Rezende; Phiala E. Shanahan
Provide a concrete, reproducible introduction to flow-based sampling for scalar and gauge lattice field theories.
Build change-of-variable flow models, train them with reverse KL using model samples, and encode lattice and U(1) gauge symmetries in coupling layers.
The document supplies executable scalar and U(1) examples and implementation guidance; it is primarily a tutorial rather than a new broad benchmark claim.
Reverse-KL self-training is mode-seeking for multimodal targets, and higher-dimensional gauge-equivariant masking remains an open design problem.
Numerical determination of the width and shape of the effective string using Stochastic Normalizing Flows (2024), authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada
Compute effective-string observables that are difficult to sample accurately with conventional local updates.
Use stochastic normalizing flows that interleave invertible layers with stochastic nonequilibrium updates.

anchor: PDF results and conclusion, around pp.15-20
supported_content: The method reproduces Nambu-Goto benchmarks and is used to study string width, flux-tube shape and beyond-leading behavior.
This is an effective-string application, not a general scaling demonstration for realistic gauge theories; more complex models remain future work.
Variational Autoregressive Networks Applied to $^4$ Field Theory Systems (2025), authors: Qian, Moxian and Chen, Shiyang
Build data-free autoregressive samplers for discrete and continuous lattice systems and reduce residual proposal bias.
Train variational autoregressive networks, add single-site/block MH corrections, and use transfer learning across coupling and size.
VAN+MH agrees with HMC on the tested Ising/phi4 systems and transfer learning reduces training epochs in the reported ranges.
Only small lattices/parameter ranges are tested; gauge theories, larger finite-size scaling and other correction mechanisms remain open.
Learning lattice quantum field theories with equivariant continuous flows (2023), authors: Gerdes, Mathis and de Haan, Pim and Rainone, Corrado and Bondesan, Roberto and Cheng, Miranda C. N.
Reduce critical-slowing and retraining costs when lattice size or theory parameters change.
Use a fully equivariant continuous flow with theory conditioning and parameter transfer across couplings and lattice sizes.
In the tested two-dimensional phi4 models, the method improves flow sampling efficiency and transfers learned parameters across nearby theories and lattice sizes.
Evidence is limited to scalar fields; gauge-theory equivariance and the training-time versus final-ESS tradeoff remain open.

Flow-based sampling for multimodal and extended-mode distributions in lattice field theory (2025), authors: Daniel C. Hackett and Chung-Chun Hsieh and Sahil Pontula and Michael S. Albergo and Denis Boyda and Jiunn-Wei Chen and Kai-Feng Chen and Kyle Cranmer and Gurtej Kanwar and Phiala E. Shanahan
Reverse-KL flow training can collapse modes or miss low-density inner tails even when global metrics look good.
Develop topology matching, equivariant/mixture architectures, forward-KL/adaptive retraining and composite HMC+flow sampling.
The tested methods alleviate mode collapse in real and complex scalar theories, while hybrid chains escape high-weight tails more reliably.
Inner-tail mismodeling persists, metrics can be deceptive, and architecture/training must be specialized; comparative cost is application dependent.
Fourier-flow model generating Feynman paths (2023), authors: Chen, Shile and Savchuk, Oleh and Zheng, Shiqi and Chen, Baoyi and Stoecker, Horst and Wang, Lingxiao and Zhou, Kai
Generate periodic Feynman paths efficiently for harmonic and double-well quantum-mechanical systems.
Combine a discrete Fourier representation with RealNVP to encode periodic path structure and use the trained Fourier flow either directly or as an MCMC proposal.
For the tested oscillators, reported low-energy observables track MCMC, while the augmented chain has acceptance above 50 percent and reduced autocorrelation.
The evidence is quantum-mechanical and low-dimensional; difficult double wells expose standalone-flow errors, and extension to QFT is future work.
Stochastic normalizing flows for Effective String Theory (2024), authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada
Apply stochastic normalizing flows to effective-string observables with reduced autocorrelation and controlled estimates.
Combine normalizing-flow transformations with stochastic updates along an interpolating protocol.
The proceedings contribution reports Binder and flux-profile observables consistent with reference expectations.
Proceedings-scale evidence and one application domain limit conclusions about general scalability.
Regressive and generative neural networks for scalar field theory (2019), authors: Zhou, Kai and Endr o di, Gergely and Pang, Long-Gang and St \"o cker, Horst
Explore whether neural networks can classify phases, predict observables, and generate configurations for a finite-density scalar lattice theory.
Train supervised regressors/classifiers and a conditional GAN on configurations generated by a conventional worm algorithm.

Within the tested scalar model, the networks recognize phases and observables beyond the training point, while generated configurations reproduce selected distributional features and a local constraint.

anchor: PDF p.1 introduction and p.8 Sec. IV
supported_content: This is a training-data-dependent proof of concept; generated samples are not an exact target sampler and extension outside the tested regime is prospective.
Sampling SU(3) pure gauge theory with Stochastic Normalizing Flows (2024), authors: Andrea Bulgarelli; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada
Mitigate critical slowing/topological freezing in 4D SU(3) while retaining favorable nonequilibrium scaling.
Insert gauge-equivariant coupling layers into a non-equilibrium Monte Carlo protocol and train by dissipated-work/KL reduction.
SNF reaches similar KL/ESS with about half the protocol steps of NE-MCMC and inherits the tested volume scaling.
Changing all links still implies n_step proportional to volume and becomes very expensive on fine lattices; protocol and architecture optimization remain open.

Aspects of scaling and scalability for flow-based sampling of lattice QCD (2022), authors: Ryan Abbott; Michael S. Albergo; Aleksandar Botev; Denis Boyda; K. Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Alexander Matthews; Sébastien Racanière; Ali Razavi; Danilo Jimenez Rezende; Fernando Romero-López; Phiala E. Shanahan; Julian M. Urban
Determine whether flow-based lattice-QCD sampling is actually scalable rather than extrapolating from toy-model ESS.
Decompose training, proposal and correction costs and analyze how architecture, transfer learning, stopping rules and protocols change scaling.
The paper shows that no single flow scaling law is meaningful; scalability must be assessed experimentally for each full algorithm.
Training cost can vary by orders of magnitude with protocol, and state-of-the-art QCD viability remains unproven.
Diffusion Models for Sampling Near Criticality in Lattice Field Theories (2026), authors: Yang-yang Tan; Gert Aarts; Diaa E. Habibi; Biagio Lucini; Lingxiao Wang
Test diffusion sampling near criticality and whether training on cheap small volumes transfers to unseen large lattices.
Use a fully convolutional reverse-SDE score model trained across volumes, plus score, MALA-acceptance and HMC-referenced ESS diagnostics.
Cross-volume models reproduce most 2D/3D phi4 observables and the propagator at unseen sizes, including L=64, with strongest residuals in infrared/zero-mode quantities.
Results use scalar theories, support filters and 2000 score evaluations; zero-mode/action-density bias, transfer range, gauge equivariance and fermions remain open.
Diffusion Models as Stochastic Quantization in Lattice Field Theory (2023), authors: Lingxiao Wang; Gert Aarts; Kai Zhou
Relate modern denoising diffusion to stochastic quantization and use it to reduce lattice-chain autocorrelation.
Learn a reverse Langevin/score process from HMC data and deploy it as a global proposal in 2D phi4.
The trained model reproduces tested configurations and reduces autocorrelation in symmetric and broken phases.
The study is a simple 2D demonstration and leaves data-free training, non-Abelian fields, fermions and complex actions unresolved.
Group-Equivariant Diffusion Models for Lattice Field Theory (2025), authors: Octavio Vega; Javad Komijani; Aida El-Khadra; Marina Marinkovic
Improve diffusion sample quality for scalar and gauge targets by encoding exact symmetries and target forces.
Use group-equivariant score networks with force-regularized score matching for 2D phi4 and U(1).
Symmetry-aware models outperform generic scores and reproduce tested observables with ESS reported between 15% and 65% in U(1) examples.
ESS decreases with volume, sampling is computationally expensive, and higher dimensions, SU(3), fermions and integrator choices remain untested.
Machine learning for four-dimensional SU(3) lattice gauge theories (2026), authors: Urs Wenger
Assess which machine-learning routes have credible prospects for 4D SU(3) lattice gauge theory.
Review normalizing/stochastic flows, diffusion processes and learned renormalization-group/fixed-point actions with physics constraints.
The review identifies physics-conditioned diffusion, stochastic nonequilibrium flows and learned fixed-point actions as promising directions with some 4D evidence.
Most approaches remain exploratory; plain normalizing flows have not scaled simultaneously to 4D, large volume and fine spacing, and physics input appears essential.

A scalable flow-based approach to mitigate topological freezing (2026), authors: Claudio Bonanno; Andrea Bulgarelli; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada; Dario Panfalone; Davide Vadacchino; Lorenzo Verzichelli
Use fast open-boundary topology dynamics while recovering periodic-boundary observables without boundary artifacts.
Train localized gauge-equivariant defect SNFs that interpolate OBC to PBC through nonequilibrium updates.
In tested 4D SU(3), defect SNF improves over NE-MCMC by about a factor of three at fixed estimator quality and reproduces topological susceptibility references.
Richer layers, optimized schedules and dynamical fermions remain open; the factor is setup specific.

Sampling QCD field configurations with gauge-equivariant flow models (2022), authors: Ryan Abbott; Michael S. Albergo; Aleksandar Botev; Denis Boyda; Kyle Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Gurtej Kanwar; Alexander G. D. G. Matthews; Sébastien Racanière; Ali Razavi; Danilo J. Rezende; Fernando Romero-López; Phiala E. Shanahan; Julian M. Urban
Test whether gauge-equivariant flows can be assembled for dynamical-fermion QCD rather than only toy gauge systems.
Combine marginal gauge and conditional pseudofermion flow models for a 4D two-flavor QCD demonstration.
On a 4^4 lattice the reweighted flow ensemble is statistically consistent with HMC for plaquette, Polyakov loop, pion correlator and topology.
The lattice is tiny; model/training choices and scaling tradeoffs are largely unexplored, and at-scale use requires major engineering/HPC integration.
The critical slowing down in diffusion models (2026), authors: Luca Maria Del Bono; Giulio Biroli; Patrick Charbonneau; Marylou Gabrié
Determine whether diffusion models automatically escape critical slowing down in training and generation.
Analyze score training and reverse generation exactly in the Gaussian O(n to infinity) model; compare one-layer, two-layer and local score architectures.

A one-layer exact-form score learns with critical slowing and transmits it to generation; a two-layer/local architecture changes training scaling from quadratic to logarithmic in system size in the analyzed setting.
The result is controlled only in the Gaussian O(n to infinity) limit; deeper architectures add overhead and finite-n/non-equilibrium systems remain open.
